ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Tom Yam Sauteed Chicken Rice. FOODON_00002682 (chicken rice dish) is the closest defensible parent class. Tom yam sauce component is not individually represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat dish with rice — FoodEx2 composite dish category used as closest defensible anchor for sauteed chicken with rice preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Tom Yam Chicken Rice. A0CKP (chicken-based composite dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not index individual prepared rice dishes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Tom Yam Sauteed Chicken Rice weighs approximately 350g, comprising roughly 200g of steamed white rice and 150g of sauteed chicken with tom yam sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Tom Yam Sauteed Chicken Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tom Yam Sauteed Chicken Rice (cr_id G00010405). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 1.2g/100g based on typical chicken thigh and palm-oil-based tom yam paste composition, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is documented as an approximation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (boiled, Asian-type)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tom Yam Sauteed Chicken Rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white rice (jasmine/Asian-type), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate component (~57% of total weight). The chicken and tom yam sauce components contribute negligible additional carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 15.12 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 38.1 / 2 ≈ 19.1 for a 350g serving containing approximately 52.9g carbohydrate. This is a conservative analogous estimate; actual GI may be modestly lower due to protein and fat co-ingestion slowing gastric emptying.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 324mg/100g from tom yam paste and fish sauce; a 350g serving delivers approximately 1,134mg sodium, representing 49–57% of the WHO daily limit of 2,000mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced tom yam paste and fish sauce, ask for sauce on the side, and limit portion size to no more than 200g to keep sodium intake below 650mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base carries a high GI of approximately 72; a standard 350g serving provides approximately 52.9g total carbohydrate, generating a glycemic load of ~19, which is in the high GL range.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute brown rice or reduce the rice portion to half, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycemic load, and monitor post-meal blood glucose as tom yam paste may contain added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 324mg/100g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is estimated at approximately 1.2g/100g (low), cholesterol is 13mg/100g (low), and trans fat is absent, which are favourable lipid characteristics.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should prioritise sodium reduction by requesting less fish sauce and tom yam paste, choose a smaller rice portion, and note that the low saturated fat and cholesterol content make this dish acceptable in moderation when sodium is controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tom yam aromatics (lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime, chilli, garlic) provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols, but chilli is a nightshade and sodium at 324mg/100g may worsen RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are nightshade-sensitive should monitor joint symptoms after consumption of chilli-containing dishes, and should request reduced fish sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Yam Sauteed Chicken Rice is a Thai-influenced Singaporean rice dish consisting of tender chicken pieces sauteed in a fragrant, tangy, and spicy tom yam sauce, served alongside or over a bed of steamed white jasmine rice. The tom yam sauce is built from a base of lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, fresh chilli, garlic, shallots, and fish sauce — aromatics that deliver a characteristic hot-sour-savoury flavour profile. The chicken is typically boneless thigh or breast meat, cooked until lightly caramelised in the sauce. Per 100g, the dish provides 116 kcal, 4.96g protein, 4.0g fat, and 15.12g carbohydrate, with sodium at 324mg — the dominant clinical concern. The white rice base carries a high glycemic index of approximately 72, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Saturated fat is low at approximately 1.2g/100g and cholesterol is modest at 13mg/100g, giving the dish a favourable lipid profile. The dish is popular as a lunch or dinner option in Singaporean hawker centres and food courts, often sold under the Y1 brand or similar hawker stall names. Clinically, the primary concerns are sodium load from fish sauce and tom yam paste (relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease) and the high-GI white rice base (relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management). The anti-inflammatory aromatics in the tom yam base — particularly lemongrass and galangal — may offer modest benefits for inflammatory conditions when consumed in moderation.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010405) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean food records. Saturated fat is estimated from typical ingredient composition as HPB does not separately report this field for this entry. GI is an analogous estimate based on the white rice component from the University of Sydney GI Database. Overall confidence is rated high due to the exact HPB match for all primary nutrients.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2g/100g) is an approximation based on typical chicken thigh and palm-oil-based tom yam paste composition; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. This value should be updated when HPB publishes a disaggregated fatty acid profile. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB database entry. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish as of April 2026. 5. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0: tom yam paste and chicken prepared by sauteing with vegetable oil do not contain industrially produced trans fats; this is confirmed as negligible/absent for this preparation type. 6. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white jasmine rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources. 7. The dish is sold under the Y1 brand in Singapore food courts; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Tom Yam Sauteed Chicken Rice' removing the brand prefix per Veritas naming rules.
